---
title: "Money and Success in Football"
author: "Md. Muktadir"
date: "`r format(Sys.Date(), '%B %d, %Y')`"
header-includes:
  - \usepackage{grffile}
  - \usepackage{float}
output:
  pdf_document:
    toc: true
    toc_depth: '1'
    latex_engine: xelatex
    number_sections: true
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_float:
      collapsed: false
      smooth_scroll: true
    toc_depth: 1
    theme: flatly
    highlight: tango
    code_folding: hide
    fig_width: 10
    fig_height: 6
    df_print: paged
    number_sections: true
subtitle: |
  Does Financial Strength Predict Points Per Game? A Generalized Additive Model
  *Sports Economics Seminar, Summer Semester 2026*
---

```{css, echo=FALSE}
/* Project colour palette */
:root {
  --navy:   #1A2A4A;
  --gold:   #F5A623;
  --red:    #C0392B;
  --green:  #196F3D;
  --steel:  #2C3E73;
  --slate:  #566573;
}

/* Callout boxes */
.callout-finding {
  background: #FEF9E7;
  border-left: 5px solid #F5A623;
  padding: 14px 18px;
  margin: 16px 0;
  border-radius: 0 6px 6px 0;
  font-size: 0.96em;
}
.callout-alert {
  background: #FDEDEC;
  border-left: 5px solid #C0392B;
  padding: 14px 18px;
  margin: 16px 0;
  border-radius: 0 6px 6px 0;
  font-size: 0.96em;
}
.callout-stat {
  background: #EBF5FB;
  border-left: 5px solid #2C3E73;
  padding: 14px 18px;
  margin: 16px 0;
  border-radius: 0 6px 6px 0;
  font-size: 0.96em;
}
.callout-success {
  background: #EAFAF1;
  border-left: 5px solid #196F3D;
  padding: 14px 18px;
  margin: 16px 0;
  border-radius: 0 6px 6px 0;
  font-size: 0.96em;
}

/* Table styling */
table.table thead tr th {
  background-color: #1A2A4A;
  color: white;
}
.best-row { font-weight: bold; background: #FEF9E7 !important; }

/* Misc */
h1, h2, h3 { color: #1A2A4A; }
.caption    { color: #566573; font-style: italic; font-size: 0.88em; }
.table-caption {
  color: #566573;
  font-style: italic;
  font-size: 0.88em;
  text-align: center;
  margin-top: 4px;
  margin-bottom: 18px;
}
```

```{r knitr-setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo      = FALSE,
  message   = FALSE,
  warning   = FALSE,
  fig.align = "center",
  fig.width = if (knitr::is_latex_output()) 6.5 else 10,
  fig.height= if (knitr::is_latex_output()) 4.5 else 6,
  dpi       = 150,
  comment   = "#>"
)
options(knitr.kable.NA = "", scipen = 6, digits = 4)
```

```{r global-load, include=FALSE}
source("utils/packages.R")
source("utils/constants.R")
source("utils/helpers.R")
load_packages(c("dplyr", "readr", "ggplot2", "broom",
                "tidyr", "scales", "purrr", "knitr", "forcats"))

ensure_installed <- function(pkgs) {
  miss <- pkgs[!vapply(pkgs, requireNamespace, logical(1), quietly = TRUE)]
  if (length(miss))
    install.packages(miss, repos = "https://cloud.r-project.org", quiet = TRUE)
  invisible(lapply(pkgs, library, character.only = TRUE))
}
ensure_installed(c("kableExtra", "ggrepel", "cowplot", "mgcv"))

kbs <- function(tbl, fw = FALSE, pos = "center",
                bo = c("striped","hover","condensed","responsive"), ...) {
  if (knitr::is_latex_output()) {
    kable_styling(tbl, full_width = FALSE,
                  latex_options = c("striped", "scale_down", "hold_position"), ...)
  } else {
    kable_styling(tbl, bootstrap_options = bo,
                  full_width = fw, position = pos, ...)
  }
}

# Table captions are printed below their table (rather than kableExtra's
# default of above) by omitting `caption =` from kbl() and instead calling
# this after the table is printed, in a chunk with results='asis'.
.tbl_n <- 0
tbl_cap <- function(text) {
  .tbl_n <<- .tbl_n + 1
  if (knitr::is_latex_output()) {
    cat(sprintf("\n\\begin{center}\\small Table %d: %s\\end{center}\n", .tbl_n, text))
  } else {
    cat(sprintf('\n<p class="table-caption">Table %d: %s</p>\n', .tbl_n, text))
  }
}

league_colours <- c(
  "Bundesliga"     = "#E41A1C",
  "La Liga"        = "#FF7F00",
  "Ligue 1"        = "#4DAF4A",
  "Premier League" = "#984EA3",
  "Serie A"        = "#377EB8"
)
league_order <- names(league_colours)
BIG4_LEAGUES <- c("Premier League", "La Liga", "Serie A", "Ligue 1")

# Position-based dataset and models (kept for corroborating context only;
# not the primary outcome in this version of the report)
df <- read_csv("data/merged/master_dataset_corrected.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) |>
  filter(!is.na(League_Position), !is.na(Financial_Rank),
         !is.na(Market_Value_M_EUR), !is.na(Log_Normalized_Value)) |>
  mutate(
    League       = factor(as.character(League), levels = league_order),
    Champion_bin = as.integer(
      tolower(as.character(Champion)) %in% c("yes", "true", "1")
    )
  )
modA <- lm(League_Position ~ Financial_Rank, data = df)
modH <- lm(League_Position ~ Financial_Rank + I(Financial_Rank^2), data = df)
n_obs      <- nrow(df)
r_pearson  <- round(cor(df$Financial_Rank, df$League_Position, use = "complete.obs"), 3)
r2_H_pos   <- round(summary(modH)$r.squared, 3)

# Points-per-game dataset and the two models adopted in this version:
# a plain linear specification and a generalized additive model with a
# smooth term on Financial Rank. Derivation matches
# extensions/scripts/16_linear_gam_revision.R exactly.
master <- read_csv("data/merged/master_dataset_corrected.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) |>
  mutate(League = as.character(League)) |>
  mutate(
    Matches_Played = case_when(
      League %in% BIG4_LEAGUES ~ 38,
      League == "Bundesliga"   ~ 34,
      TRUE                     ~ NA_real_
    ),
    Points_Per_Game = Points / Matches_Played
  )
model_df_ppg <- master |> filter(!is.na(Financial_Rank), !is.na(Points_Per_Game))

lin_ppg <- lm(Points_Per_Game ~ Financial_Rank, data = model_df_ppg)
gam_ppg <- mgcv::gam(Points_Per_Game ~ s(Financial_Rank), data = model_df_ppg, method = "REML")

lin_summary <- summary(lin_ppg)
gam_summary <- summary(gam_ppg)
gam_edf     <- unname(gam_summary$s.table[1, "edf"])
gam_devexpl <- gam_summary$dev.expl

aug_gam <- augment(gam_ppg, data = model_df_ppg) |>
  rename(Fitted_PPG = .fitted, Residual_PPG = .resid)
resid_sd_gam   <- sd(aug_gam$Residual_PPG, na.rm = TRUE)
overp_thr_gam  <-  1.5 * resid_sd_gam
underp_thr_gam <- -1.5 * resid_sd_gam
aug_gam <- aug_gam |>
  mutate(Outlier_Flag = case_when(
    Residual_PPG > overp_thr_gam  ~ "Overperformer",
    Residual_PPG < underp_thr_gam ~ "Underperformer",
    TRUE                          ~ "Normal"
  ))

aug_lin <- augment(lin_ppg, data = model_df_ppg) |>
  rename(Fitted_PPG_lin = .fitted, Residual_PPG_lin = .resid)
resid_sd_lin <- sd(aug_lin$Residual_PPG_lin, na.rm = TRUE)

leicester_gam  <- aug_gam |> filter(grepl("Leicester", Team), Season == "2015-2016")
leverkusen_gam <- aug_gam |> filter(grepl("Leverkusen", Team), Season == "2023-2024")

# Existing project outputs read directly, not recomputed
desc_raw    <- read_csv("outputs/01_descriptive_statistics.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)
coef_H_raw  <- read_csv("outputs/20_quadratic_model_results.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)
coef_all    <- read_csv("outputs/08_regression_results.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)
champ_csv   <- read_csv("outputs/31_all_champions_with_residuals.csv", show_col_types = FALSE) |>
  mutate(League = factor(as.character(League), levels = league_order))
gam_table   <- read_csv("extensions/outputs/gam_revision/gam_revision_table.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)
e1_table    <- read_csv("extensions/outputs/E1_model_evolution_table.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)
nba_summary <- read_csv("outputs/40_nba_vs_football_summary.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)
hsk_tests   <- read_csv("outputs/14_heteroskedasticity_autocorrelation_tests.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)
robust_se   <- read_csv("outputs/15_modelD_robust_se.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)

n_champ    <- nrow(champ_csv)
fr1_champs <- sum(champ_csv$Financial_Rank == 1)
pct_fr1    <- round(100 * fr1_champs / n_champ)
fr_gt3_n   <- sum(champ_csv$Financial_Rank > 3)
```


# Abstract {-}

This report investigates whether financial strength predicts on-field
success in European football, using points per game as the outcome and a
generalized additive model (GAM) to let the relationship with Financial
Rank take a flexible, data-driven shape rather than a fixed linear or
polynomial form. Drawing on `r format(n_obs, big.mark=",")` team-seasons
across five major European leagues (Bundesliga, La Liga, Ligue 1, Premier
League, Serie A) over the 2015-16 to 2024-25 seasons, the model finds that
Financial Rank explains **`r round(100*gam_devexpl)`%** of the variation
in points earned per match (deviance explained = `r round(gam_devexpl,3)`).
The same relationship, measured against final league position using a
Pearson correlation, yields r = `r r_pearson` (R squared =
`r r2_H_pos`), confirming that money is a strong predictor of sporting
success under either outcome measure. Two clubs remain the standout
exceptions to this pattern across every specification tested in this
report: Leicester City's 2015-16 Premier League title and Bayer
Leverkusen's unbeaten 2023-24 Bundesliga title, both of which earned far
more points per game than their financial rank predicted.

# Introduction

## Why Does This Question Matter?

The five major European football leagues (the English Premier League,
Spanish La Liga, German Bundesliga, Italian Serie A, and French Ligue 1)
collectively generate over 18 billion euros in annual revenue, with squad
transfer spending exceeding 12 billion euros in recent seasons. With this
much money flowing through the sport, a natural and important question
arises: does financial investment actually buy sporting success, or can a
well organised, under funded club systematically compete at the top level?

This question reaches beyond football itself. Competitive balance policy
is one reason it matters: UEFA introduced Financial Fair Play specifically
to prevent the wealthiest clubs from buying their way to dominance, and if
money truly determines outcomes, such policies become more important, not
less. Fan engagement is a second reason: if league tables are financially
predetermined, some of the drama of competition is lost for supporters of
smaller clubs. Sports economics theory is a third reason: understanding how
resource advantages translate into tournament outcomes informs similar
questions across every competitive sport with unequal team budgets.

## The Analytical Approach Taken in This Report

Two methodological choices shape everything that follows, and both are set
out here at the start rather than introduced partway through.

The first choice is the outcome variable. Rather than final league
position (a ranking from first to last), this report treats points per
game as the outcome to be explained. League position is what statisticians
call an ordinal variable: it tells us that a team finished third and not
fourth, but it says nothing about how large that gap actually was. A club
that misses the title by a single point looks identical, position-wise, to
one that misses it by twenty. Points per game is a continuous, evenly
spaced quantity instead: the difference between 1.50 and 1.60 points per
game always means the same thing, regardless of where in the table a team
sits. A continuous outcome is generally the better fit for the regression
methods used throughout this report, while a purely ordinal outcome like
raw position is better suited to specialised rank based methods. Points
per game also solves a second, more practical problem: because the
Bundesliga plays a 34 match season while the other four leagues play 38
matches, raw points totals are not directly comparable across leagues.
Dividing by matches played removes that distortion (this is explained
further in the Data section).

The second choice is the functional form connecting financial strength to
performance, that is, the shape of the mathematical relationship between
the two. Earlier stages of this project fitted a fixed, curved shape (a
quadratic term) chosen in advance. This report instead fits a generalized
additive model, which allows the data to determine how much the
relationship should curve and where, rather than committing to one
particular curved shape before looking at the data. Both of these choices,
the outcome variable and the modelling approach, are explained in full,
plain language detail in the Methodology section below; they are simply
stated here as the analytical plan followed throughout the rest of this
report.

## What We Set Out to Answer

This report addresses three related questions using ten years of data from
all five top tier European leagues. First, direction and strength: is
there a statistically meaningful relationship between a club's financial
rank within its league and how many points per game it earns, and how
strong is that relationship? Second, model form: does a straight line
relationship capture the pattern in the data, or is a more flexible,
curved relationship needed, and does the data support a smoothly varying
effect beyond what a single fixed curve could represent? Third, exceptions:
which clubs defy the financial hierarchy, and how statistically unusual are
they under this revised approach?


# The Data We Used

## Sources and Sample

The dataset combines two sources. ESPN FC provided final league standings
and total points won for all five leagues across the 2015-16 through
2024-25 seasons. Transfermarkt provided squad market values, the estimated
total transfer market worth of all players in a squad, at the start of
each season. After merging these sources and removing rows with missing
key values, the working dataset contains `r format(n_obs, big.mark=",")`
team-season observations; every club that played a full season in one of
the five leagues during the study period appears once per season. One
Serie A observation, SPAL in 2017-18, had a missing market value in the
original source due to a merge failure between the standings and market
value data; the correct value of 63.55 million euros was verified
directly on Transfermarkt and inserted, raising N from 975 to 976.

## Financial Rank: Why Rank Instead of Raw Value

Squad market values cannot be compared directly across leagues. A squad
worth 300 million euros is the richest in Ligue 1 but only the
fifth-richest in the Premier League, since the Premier League's overall
spending is much higher. Comparing raw euro values across leagues would be
an apples to oranges comparison. The solution used throughout this project
is Financial Rank: each squad is ranked from 1 (the most valuable squad)
to the number of clubs in that league that season, separately within each
league and season. Financial Rank 1 always means "the wealthiest club in
this specific league this specific season," regardless of whether that
squad is worth 600 million euros or 150 million euros. This within-league
ranking is what makes it possible to pool all five leagues into a single
analysis.

## Points Per Game: Why Divide by Matches Played

The outcome variable in this report, points per game, is total points
earned in a season divided by matches played that season. This division
matters because not every league plays the same number of matches: the
Bundesliga has 18 clubs and a 34 match season, while the other four
leagues have 20 clubs and a 38 match season. A club in a 38 match league
can accumulate up to 114 points (38 wins at 3 points each), while a club
in the 34 match Bundesliga can accumulate at most 102 points. Comparing
raw point totals across these two league formats would systematically and
unfairly penalise Bundesliga clubs. Dividing by matches played converts
every team-season onto the same scale (points earned per match played),
making comparisons across league formats fair for the first time.

## Descriptive Statistics

```{r desc-stats-combined, results='asis'}
desc_raw |>
  mutate(League = ifelse(Group == "ALL LEAGUES (combined)", "All leagues (combined)", Group)) |>
  select(League, N, Mean_Position, Mean_MV_M_EUR, Mean_Points, Corr_FR_Pos) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(., 2))) |>
  kbl(longtable = FALSE,
    col.names = c("League", "N", "Mean position", "Mean squad value (million euros)",
                  "Mean points", "Pearson r (FR vs. position)")
  ) |>
  kbs() |>
  row_spec(0, bold = TRUE, background = "#1A2A4A", color = "white") |>
  row_spec(1, bold = TRUE, background = "#EBF5FB")
tbl_cap("Sample size, average outcomes, and the Financial Rank to position correlation, by league and combined.")
```

The "All leagues (combined)" row covers the full sample. Squad values vary
substantially by league (the Premier League's average is considerably
higher than Ligue 1's, for example), while the raw correlation between
Financial Rank and final position already exceeds 0.78 in every single
league before any model is fitted, reaching 0.89 in Serie A. This is a
first hint, well before the modelling begins, that financial strength is a
powerful predictor of sporting outcomes in every one of the five leagues
studied.


# Methodology: From a Straight Line to a Learned Curve

This report builds its model in three deliberate steps, each one relaxing
an assumption made by the step before it. Each step is explained in full
before the next one is introduced, so that a reader who has never
encountered a generalized additive model can follow the reasoning from
first principles.

## Step 1: A Straight Line (Linear Specification)

The simplest possible model is a straight line: predicted points per game
equals some baseline value, plus a fixed amount for every step down the
financial hierarchy. This kind of relationship is called linear because,
if plotted, it forms a single straight line. A linear model implies a
strong assumption: that the penalty for being one Financial Rank worse is
exactly the same everywhere, whether we are comparing the wealthiest club
to the second wealthiest, or the fourteenth wealthiest to the fifteenth.
There is no reason to expect that assumption to be true in general, but it
is the natural starting point and a useful baseline against which to judge
whether anything more complex is actually needed.

**Model equation:**

$$\text{PPG}_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot \text{FR}_i + \varepsilon_i$$

where $\text{PPG}_i$ is points per game for club-season $i$, $\text{FR}_i$
is that club-season's Financial Rank, $\beta_0$ is the intercept (the
predicted points per game at Financial Rank zero, a reference point rather
than an observed case), $\beta_1$ is the slope (the change in predicted
points per game for every one-rank drop in Financial Rank, expected to be
negative), and $\varepsilon_i$ is the residual, the gap between a club's
actual and predicted points per game left unexplained by the line.

**R implementation:**

```r
lin_ppg <- lm(Points_Per_Game ~ Financial_Rank, data = model_df_ppg)
```

`lm()` fits the equation above by ordinary least squares, choosing
$\beta_0$ and $\beta_1$ to minimise the sum of squared residuals;
`Points_Per_Game ~ Financial_Rank` is R's formula shorthand for "regress
points per game on Financial Rank."

## Step 2: A Fixed Curve (Quadratic Specification)

Earlier stages of this project (documented in full in the Appendix)
established that the straight line assumption does not hold: the penalty
for a worse financial rank is larger at the top of the hierarchy (between
the first and second richest clubs, for example) than at the bottom
(between the nineteenth and twentieth richest). To capture that pattern,
a quadratic term was added: alongside Financial Rank itself, the model
also includes Financial Rank multiplied by itself (Financial Rank
squared). A squared term lets the fitted line bend into a single smooth
curve, specifically a parabola, rather than staying straight. This is a
genuine improvement, but it still commits to one particular curved shape
in advance: a parabola always has exactly one bend, always symmetric on
either side. There is no substantive reason to expect that the true
relationship between financial rank and performance takes exactly this
shape, rather than, for instance, levelling off among mid table clubs
before steepening again near the bottom of the table.

**Model equation:**

$$\text{PPG}_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot \text{FR}_i + \beta_2 \cdot \text{FR}_i^2 + \varepsilon_i$$

where $\text{PPG}_i$, $\text{FR}_i$, $\beta_0$, $\beta_1$, and
$\varepsilon_i$ carry the same meaning as in Step 1, and $\beta_2$ is the
coefficient on Financial Rank squared, the new term that lets the fitted
relationship bend into a parabola rather than stay straight. This is the
specification referred to as Model H elsewhere in this report.

**R implementation:**

```r
quad_ppg <- lm(Points_Per_Game ~ Financial_Rank + I(Financial_Rank^2),
                data = model_df_ppg)
```

`I(Financial_Rank^2)` tells R to treat Financial Rank squared as its own
predictor column (the `I()` wrapper prevents `^` from being read as
formula shorthand); `lm()` then estimates $\beta_0$, $\beta_1$, and
$\beta_2$ jointly by ordinary least squares, exactly as in Step 1 but with
one additional term.

## Step 3: A Learned Curve (Generalized Additive Model)

The final step, and the model adopted for the remainder of this report,
relaxes the quadratic assumption entirely. A generalized additive model
(commonly abbreviated GAM) replaces the fixed squared term with a smooth
term: a flexible curve built from a combination of simpler basis
functions, whose exact shape is estimated from the data rather than
chosen by the analyst in advance. In plain terms, instead of telling the
model "the relationship must be a parabola" and asking it to find the best
parabola, a generalized additive model is told "find whatever smooth curve
fits best," and it is free to produce a shape that bends more in one place
than another, flattens out, or steepens, wherever the data actually
support that pattern. The model fitted here is points per game explained
by a smooth function of Financial Rank, estimated using the mgcv package
in R with restricted maximum likelihood smoothing parameter selection (a
standard, data driven method for choosing how flexible the smooth curve is
allowed to be, balancing fit against overfitting). This preserves the core
intuition behind the quadratic term, that the effect of financial rank is
not constant, while removing the requirement that the non-constant effect
take one specific, predetermined shape.

**Model equation:**

$$\text{PPG}_i = \beta_0 + f(\text{FR}_i) + \varepsilon_i, \qquad
f(\text{FR}) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} b_k(\text{FR}) \, \gamma_k$$

where $\text{PPG}_i$, $\text{FR}_i$, $\beta_0$, and $\varepsilon_i$ carry
the same meaning as in Steps 1 and 2, and $f(\cdot)$ is the smooth term: an
unspecified curve built from $K$ spline basis functions $b_k(\cdot)$ with
weights $\gamma_k$ estimated from the data, rather than a term whose shape
(straight line, parabola) is fixed in advance. A penalty on how much
$f(\cdot)$ is allowed to wiggle is controlled by a smoothing parameter,
chosen automatically by restricted maximum likelihood (REML) so that the
curve's flexibility is set by the data rather than by the analyst.

**R implementation:**

```r
gam_ppg <- mgcv::gam(Points_Per_Game ~ s(Financial_Rank), data = model_df_ppg,
                      method = "REML")
```

`s(Financial_Rank)` is the `mgcv` syntax for "fit a smooth term on
Financial Rank" rather than a single linear or quadratic coefficient;
`method = "REML"` selects the smoothing-parameter estimation method
described above. Everything downstream in this report (fitted values,
residuals, outlier flags) is computed from this fitted `gam_ppg` object.


# Core Results

## The Three-Way Comparison

The table below compares all three specifications from the Methodology
section: the linear model, the quadratic model (labelled Model H
throughout this project and its earlier reports), and the generalized
additive model, all fitted with points per game as the outcome and
Financial Rank as the only predictor. Three columns need explaining before
the numbers are presented. The first is R squared or deviance explained:
for the two ordinary regression models this is R squared, the share of
the variation in points per game the model accounts for, from 0 (none) to
1 (all of it); for the generalized additive model the comparable quantity
is deviance explained, which measures the same idea (share of variation
accounted for) but is computed differently because a GAM's predictions do
not come from a simple sum of fixed coefficients. The second column is
AIC (Akaike Information Criterion), a score that rewards a model for
fitting the data well but penalises it for using unnecessary complexity;
lower AIC indicates a better trade-off between accuracy and simplicity,
and AIC is only meaningful for comparing models fitted to the exact same
outcome variable and data, which is the case for all three rows here. The
third column, effective degrees of freedom, is reported only for the
generalized additive model and is explained fully below the table.

```{r core-results-table, results='asis'}
gam_table |>
  mutate(Model = recode(Model,
    "H: Quadratic (PPG) [reference]" = "Quadratic (Model H)",
    "Linear (PPG)"                  = "Linear",
    "GAM (PPG)"                     = "Generalized additive model"
  )) |>
  select(Model, N, R2_or_Dev_Explained, AIC, EDF) |>
  kbl(longtable = FALSE,
    col.names = c("Model", "N", "R squared / deviance explained", "AIC",
                  "Effective degrees of freedom")
  ) |>
  kbs() |>
  row_spec(0, bold = TRUE, background = "#1A2A4A", color = "white") |>
  row_spec(3, bold = TRUE, background = "#FEF9E7")
tbl_cap("Comparison of the linear, quadratic, and generalized additive specifications for points per game, from this report's underlying GAM revision output.")
```

Moving from a straight line to the fixed quadratic curve already raises
the explained variation from `r round(gam_table$R2_or_Dev_Explained[gam_table$Model=="Linear (PPG)"],3)`
to `r round(gam_table$R2_or_Dev_Explained[gam_table$Model=="H: Quadratic (PPG) [reference]"],3)`
and lowers AIC by well over a hundred points. Moving further, from the
fixed quadratic curve to the learned curve, raises the explained variation
again, to a deviance explained of `r round(gam_devexpl,3)`, and lowers AIC
again, to `r round(AIC(gam_ppg),2)`. Both curved specifications outperform
the straight line comfortably, and the generalized additive model in turn
outperforms the fixed quadratic curve on both statistics at once, using a
comparable number of effective parameters.

## Effective Degrees of Freedom, Explained

Effective degrees of freedom (edf) measures how much curvature the smooth
term actually used, on a scale where 1 means the smooth term behaved
exactly like a straight line, and larger values mean progressively more
flexible, wigglier curves. If the effective degrees of freedom here had
come out close to 1, that would mean the extra flexibility of a
generalized additive model bought nothing beyond what the simple linear
model already achieved. Instead, the effective degrees of freedom is
`r round(gam_edf,2)`, well above 1, which confirms there is genuine,
non-trivial curvature in the relationship between financial rank and
points per game that a straight line would completely miss. At the same
time, `r round(gam_edf,2)` is also more nuanced than the two degrees of
freedom implied by a fixed quadratic term: a quadratic curve can only bend
once, in one direction, whereas a smooth term with an effective degrees of
freedom around `r round(gam_edf,0)` can accommodate a more gradual,
multi-part curve, for example a steep early drop among the wealthiest
clubs followed by a flatter, more gradual decline among the rest. In
short, the data support meaningfully more curvature than a single parabola
allows, without requiring anywhere near the flexibility of an
unconstrained, highly irregular curve.

## The Fitted Curve and Its Confidence Band

```{r gam-smooth-fig, out.width="80%", fig.height=3.8, fig.pos="H", fig.cap="The generalized additive model's fitted smooth term (dark line) and 95 percent confidence band (shaded), plotted against Financial Rank, with individual team-seasons jittered by league in the background."}
knitr::include_graphics("extensions/outputs/gam_revision/gam_smooth_confidence.png")
```

The shaded region around the fitted curve is a confidence band, the
two-dimensional version of a confidence interval. A confidence interval is
a range of values, calculated from the data, that is expected to contain
the true underlying value with a stated degree of certainty, conventionally
95%; a confidence band simply plots that same range at every point along
the curve, so its width shows exactly where the estimate is precise and
where it is uncertain. Reading the figure: points per game falls steeply
as Financial Rank worsens from first to roughly tenth, then flattens into
a shallower decline for the remaining, less wealthy clubs, exactly the
kind of multi-part curvature the effective degrees of freedom result
predicted above.

One limitation is visible in the same figure: the confidence band is
narrow through most of the range but widens noticeably for financial ranks
above approximately 15. This is a sample size effect, not a weakness in
the modelling approach. The Bundesliga fields only eighteen clubs per
season, so Financial Ranks of 19 and 20 are only ever populated by the
other four leagues, each of which has twenty clubs. The result is a
genuinely smaller number of observations at the poorest end of the
financial rank scale, and the wider band there is an honest reflection of
that reduced sample size, not evidence that the model performs worse for
those clubs.


# Outlier Analysis

## What a Residual Is, and Why It Is Useful

Once a model has been fitted, every team-season has a predicted value
(what the model expects that club to earn in points per game, given its
Financial Rank) and an actual value (what the club actually earned). The
difference between the two, actual minus predicted, is called the
residual. A residual close to zero means the model predicted that club
well. A large positive residual means a club earned far more points per
game than its finances predicted, an overperformer. A large negative
residual means a club earned far fewer points per game than its finances
predicted, an underperformer. Looking at the most extreme residuals is a
systematic way of finding the clubs whose season cannot be explained by
money alone, exactly the stories that make football interesting.

This report flags any team-season whose residual is more than 1.5
standard deviations from zero (a standard deviation is a measure of how
spread out the residuals typically are; 1.5 times that spread marks a
genuinely unusual case, not just ordinary season to season noise) as a
statistical outlier. Under the generalized additive model, the residual
standard deviation is `r round(resid_sd_gam,3)` points per game, so the
outlier threshold is `r round(overp_thr_gam,3)` points per game in either
direction. As an additional check on how much a single unusual
observation can distort a fitted curve, this project also computed each
observation's Cook's distance, a measure of how much the entire fitted
model would shift if that one observation were removed; consistent with
the position based analysis in earlier stages of this project, Leicester
City's 2015-16 season is again the single most influential observation
under the revised model (full diagnostic values are in the Appendix).

## The Ten Largest Overperformers and Underperformers

```{r outlier-table-export, include=FALSE}
outlier_tbl_full <- aug_gam |>
  filter(Outlier_Flag != "Normal") |>
  mutate(League = factor(League, levels = league_order)) |>
  select(Team, Season, League, Financial_Rank, Points_Per_Game, Fitted_PPG, Residual_PPG, Outlier_Flag) |>
  arrange(desc(abs(Residual_PPG))) |>
  mutate(across(c(Points_Per_Game, Fitted_PPG, Residual_PPG), ~ round(., 3)))

readr::write_csv(outlier_tbl_full, "extensions/outputs/gam_revision/flagged_outliers_full.csv")

top_over  <- outlier_tbl_full |> filter(Outlier_Flag == "Overperformer")  |> arrange(desc(Residual_PPG))       |> slice_head(n = 10)
top_under <- outlier_tbl_full |> filter(Outlier_Flag == "Underperformer") |> arrange(desc(abs(Residual_PPG))) |> slice_head(n = 10)
```

```{r outlier-table-over, results='asis'}
top_over |>
  select(-Outlier_Flag) |>
  kbl(longtable = FALSE,
    col.names = c("Team", "Season", "League", "FR", "Actual PPG",
                  "Pred. PPG", "Residual")
  ) |>
  kbs() |>
  row_spec(0, bold = TRUE, background = "#1A2A4A", color = "white") |>
  row_spec(1:nrow(top_over), background = "#EBF5FB")
tbl_cap("Top 10 overperformers under the generalized additive model, sorted by residual size (largest positive first). FR is Financial Rank; PPG is points per game.")
```

```{r outlier-table-under, results='asis'}
top_under |>
  select(-Outlier_Flag) |>
  kbl(longtable = FALSE,
    col.names = c("Team", "Season", "League", "FR", "Actual PPG",
                  "Pred. PPG", "Residual")
  ) |>
  kbs() |>
  row_spec(0, bold = TRUE, background = "#1A2A4A", color = "white") |>
  row_spec(1:nrow(top_under), background = "#FDEDEC")
tbl_cap("Top 10 underperformers under the generalized additive model, sorted by absolute residual size (largest negative first). FR is Financial Rank; PPG is points per game.")
```

The two tables above show the ten largest overperformers and ten largest
underperformers; the complete table of all `r nrow(outlier_tbl_full)`
flagged team-seasons is available as a CSV file in the project repository
under `extensions/outputs/gam_revision/flagged_outliers_full.csv`.

## Leicester City 2015-16: A Closer Look, Restated in Points Per Game

Leicester City's Premier League title in 2015-16 is widely regarded as one
of the greatest upsets in sporting history, and it remains exactly that
under the revised model. Leicester finished Financial Rank 11 out of 20
that season, yet won the title outright. Under the generalized additive
model, Leicester's actual points per game exceeded the model's prediction
by `r round(leicester_gam$Residual_PPG,3)` points per game
(`r round(leicester_gam$Residual_PPG / resid_sd_gam,1)` standard
deviations above expectation), the single largest overperformance in the
entire dataset under this specification, larger in relative terms than it
was under the earlier linear model. Bayer Leverkusen's unbeaten 2023-24
Bundesliga title shows the same pattern: an overperformance of
`r round(leverkusen_gam$Residual_PPG,3)` points per game relative to what
their financial rank predicted. Both clubs continue to stand out as
genuine statistical anomalies regardless of whether the outcome is
measured in league position, total points, or points per game, and
regardless of whether the functional form connecting financial rank to
performance is a straight line, a fixed curve, or a learned curve. That
consistency across every specification tried in this project is itself
evidence that these two results are real football stories, not artefacts
of any one modelling choice.

## Champions from Outside the Financial Top Three

This next result depends only on Financial Rank, not on which outcome
variable is used to measure performance, so it is carried over unchanged
from earlier stages of this project. Of `r n_champ` league titles across
the decade studied, `r pct_fr1`% (`r fr1_champs` clubs) were won by the
financially wealthiest squad in their league that season, and only
`r fr_gt3_n` champions came from outside the financial top three.

```{r champions-fr-dist-fig, out.width="75%", fig.height=3.8, fig.pos="H", fig.cap="Distribution of Financial Rank among all 50 league champions across 10 seasons and 5 leagues. The dominance of Financial Rank 1 is striking."}
knitr::include_graphics("outputs/34_champions_fr_dist.png")
```

Of the `r fr_gt3_n` champions from outside the top three, three (Napoli at
Financial Rank 5, Lille at Financial Rank 4, and Liverpool at Financial
Rank 4) were only barely outside the expected zone and within a normal
range of variation. Only Leicester City, at Financial Rank 11, is a
genuine statistical outlier among the champions, confirming the finding
above.


# Robustness

## Why Run the Same Analysis Under Different Conditions

A single result, however strong, could in principle be an artefact of one
particular time period, one particular league, or one particular sport's
institutional rules. A robustness check re-runs the same core analysis
under a different slice of the data, or on a genuinely different dataset,
to see whether the same conclusion still holds. If it does, that is strong
evidence the finding reflects something real about how money and
performance relate, rather than a coincidence of the specific sample
used. The checks summarised below were originally carried out on the
position based specification developed earlier in this project (full
detail, including formal statistical tests such as checks for
heteroskedasticity, a situation in which the spread of a model's errors is
not constant across the range of predictions, and the corresponding use of
robust standard errors, standard error estimates adjusted to remain valid
even when that spread is not constant, is preserved in the Appendix). The
fact that the same pattern holds under a different outcome variable
entirely (points per game modelled with a generalized additive model, the
approach used throughout this report) makes the overall finding more
trustworthy still, since it means the conclusion does not depend on the
specific choice of how success is measured.

## Consistency Across Leagues

```{r league-consistency-fig, out.width="75%", fig.height=3.5, fig.pos="H", fig.cap="R-squared (variance explained in final position) by Financial Rank, broken down by league."}
knitr::include_graphics("outputs/32_league_r2_bars.png")
```

Financial Rank predicts final position with an R squared between 0.61 and
0.79 depending on the league, with Serie A showing the strongest
relationship and the Bundesliga and Ligue 1 the weakest, though even the
weakest of the five remains a strong relationship by ordinary social
science standards. No single league is driving the overall result; the
financial hierarchy operates similarly across all five leagues studied.

## Stability Over the Decade Studied

```{r time-split-fig, out.width="75%", fig.height=3.5, fig.pos="H", fig.cap="Financial Rank vs. League Position, fit separately for the Early (2015-16 to 2019-20) and Late (2020-21 to 2024-25) halves of the decade. The two fitted curves are visually almost indistinguishable."}
knitr::include_graphics("outputs/38a_time_split_scatter.png")
```

Splitting the ten seasons into two independent five-season halves and
re-fitting the model separately on each half produces two fitted curves
that overlap almost completely (Early period Financial Rank slope
`r round(1.2497,2)`, Late period slope `r round(1.2102,2)`; a formal test
of whether the two slopes differ gives p = 0.797, far from statistically
significant). The relationship between financial rank and performance has
neither strengthened nor weakened across the decade studied.

## Transfer Market Inflation

Squad market values rose substantially over the decade studied: indexed
to a 2019-20 base of 1.00, the average squad value across the five
leagues stood at 0.59 in 2015-16 and had risen to 1.30 by 2024-25. This
raises a natural question: does that inflation distort Financial Rank, or
any of the results built on it?

By construction, it does not. Financial Rank is computed separately
within each league-season: every squad in the same league-season is
ranked against the other squads in that same league-season only, never
against squads from a different year. Because inflation raises all squad
values within a given league-season together, it scales every club by
roughly the same factor at the same point in time. A within-season rank
is unaffected by a common scaling: if every value in a league-season is
multiplied by the same number, the order from richest to poorest does not
change, so neither does the rank.

This was also checked directly rather than left as a theoretical
argument. Every squad market value in the corrected dataset was deflated
by the yearly inflation index, and Financial Rank was recomputed from
those deflated values for all 976 team-seasons. The recomputed ranks were
identical to the original ranks in every single case: zero rank changes
across the full corrected dataset. This directly addresses a question
raised after the presentation of this project, whether the headline
result could be an artefact of rising transfer market prices over the
decade rather than a genuine, stable relationship between financial
strength and sporting performance; the answer is that it is not.

## Beyond Football: A Salary-Capped League

```{r nba-comparison-fig, out.width="80%", fig.height=3.8, fig.pos="H", fig.cap="Side by side comparison: Financial Rank vs. League Position (European football, no salary cap) and Payroll Rank vs. Final Standing (NBA, hard salary cap)."}
knitr::include_graphics("outputs/40_nba_vs_football_comparison.png")
```

The same quadratic specification, fit on ten seasons of NBA data (a
professional basketball league that, unlike European football, operates
under a hard salary cap limiting how much any one team can spend on
player wages), confirms that the money predicts success pattern is not
unique to football: Payroll Rank significantly predicts NBA final
standing. But the relationship is far weaker under the NBA's cap
(R squared = `r nba_summary$R2[2]`, r = `r nba_summary$Pearson_r[2]`)
than in football's largely unrestricted spending environment
(R squared = `r nba_summary$R2[1]`, r = `r nba_summary$Pearson_r[1]`), and
only `r nba_summary$Pct_Top3_Payroll_Titles[2]`% of NBA titles in the
period went to a top three payroll team, versus
`r nba_summary$Pct_Top3_Payroll_Titles[1]`% in football. This is indirect
evidence that deliberate cap style mechanisms, such as the salary cap
tested here or Financial Fair Play in European football, can meaningfully
weaken, without eliminating, the link between spending and winning.

::: {.callout-success}
**Points per game, checked against total points, was already tested and held.**
An earlier stage of this project also re-fit the quadratic specification
with total points won as the outcome instead of final position, and found
that Leicester City 2015-16 remained the single largest statistical
anomaly under that outcome too. Combined with the results above, the core
finding has now been confirmed under three different outcome variables
(position, points, and points per game) and two different functional
forms (a fixed quadratic curve and a learned smooth curve), in addition
to the time split and cross sport checks in this section.
:::


# Conclusions

This report set out to determine whether financial strength predicts
sporting success in Europe's five major football leagues, using points per
game as the outcome and a generalized additive model to let the data
determine the shape of that relationship. The answer is unambiguous: yes,
and very strongly so. Each finding below is written to stand on its own.

::: {.callout-finding}
**Finding 1: Financial Rank explains most of the variation in points per
game, and does so better than any fixed-shape alternative tried.** The
generalized additive model achieves a deviance explained of
`r round(gam_devexpl,3)` (`r round(100*gam_devexpl)`%) and the lowest AIC
of the three specifications compared, `r round(AIC(gam_ppg),2)`. The same
underlying relationship, measured the original way through final league
position, gives a Pearson correlation of r = `r r_pearson` and
R squared = `r r2_H_pos`, confirming the result under an earlier,
independently developed specification.
:::

::: {.callout-finding}
**Finding 2: The relationship genuinely curves, and the curve is more
nuanced than a simple parabola.** The generalized additive model's
effective degrees of freedom of `r round(gam_edf,2)`, well above the value
of 1 that would indicate no curvature at all, confirms real, non-trivial
curvature in how financial rank relates to performance, while also
showing that the curve is more flexible than the single symmetric bend a
fixed quadratic term allows. In plain terms, the gap between the richest
and second richest club matters more than the gap between two closely
matched mid table clubs, and the shape of that pattern is best estimated
directly from the data rather than assumed in advance.
:::

::: {.callout-finding}
**Finding 3: The relationship is consistent across leagues and stable
over time.** Financial Rank predicts final position with an R squared
between 0.61 and 0.79 in every one of the five leagues studied, and
splitting the decade into two independent five-season halves produces
statistically indistinguishable fitted curves (p = 0.797 for the test of
whether the slopes differ). Financial dominance is a stable, general
feature of European football during the period studied, not a
league-specific or recent phenomenon.
:::

::: {.callout-alert}
**Finding 4: Genuine exceptions exist, but only two are truly
extraordinary, and both survive every specification tested.** Of
`r n_champ` league titles across the decade, only `r fr_gt3_n` were won by
clubs outside the financial top three, and only Leicester City 2015-16 is
a genuine statistical outlier among them. Under the generalized additive
model adopted in this report, Leicester's overperformance grows to
`r round(leicester_gam$Residual_PPG / resid_sd_gam,1)` standard deviations
above expectation, and Bayer Leverkusen's unbeaten 2023-24 title joins it
as a second clear overperformer. Both results hold under every outcome
variable and every functional form tested in this project.
:::

::: {.callout-finding}
**Finding 5: The result survives a battery of independent robustness
checks.** The relationship holds within every individual league, across
both halves of the decade studied, under three different outcome
variables (position, points, and points per game), and under two
different functional forms (a fixed quadratic curve and a learned smooth
curve). No single check weakens the core finding.
:::

::: {.callout-finding}
**Finding 6: The pattern generalises beyond football, but a salary cap
visibly weakens it.** The same specification fit on NBA data confirms
that money predicts success is not football-specific, but the
relationship is far weaker under the NBA's hard salary cap
(R squared = `r nba_summary$R2[2]`) than in football's largely
unrestricted spending environment (R squared = `r nba_summary$R2[1]`).
This is indirect evidence that deliberate cap style mechanisms can
meaningfully loosen, without eliminating, the link between spending and
winning.
:::

## Limitations and Future Directions

```{r limitations-table, results='asis'}
tibble(
  Limitation = c(
    "Market value as a quality proxy",
    "No managerial quality variable",
    "No injury data",
    "Panel structure ignored",
    "Top divisions only",
    "NBA comparison is a single, small sample",
    "Confidence intervals widen at the sparse end of Financial Rank"
  ),
  Issue = c(
    "Transfermarkt valuations may lag actual squad quality by weeks and reflect subjective editor estimates",
    "Exceptional coaches (such as Claudio Ranieri at Leicester) are not captured in the financial model",
    "Injury crises can dramatically alter a squad's effective quality mid-season",
    "Teams appear across multiple seasons; correlated errors across seasons are unmodelled",
    "Results may not generalise to lower divisions or smaller national leagues",
    "The NBA cross-sport comparison rests on only 300 team-seasons and 10 champion-seasons, and NBA titles are playoff-determined rather than table position, making the sample smaller and structurally different from the football data",
    "The generalized additive model's confidence band widens noticeably for Financial Ranks above approximately 15, a direct consequence of the Bundesliga's 18-club format leaving fewer observations at the poorest end of the financial rank scale"
  ),
  Potential_Fix = c(
    "Use wage bill data as an alternative, or cross-validate with an independent squad rating source",
    "Add manager tenure, prior record, or a coaching quality index",
    "Incorporate injury-adjusted squad value, for example weighted by player availability",
    "Fixed-effects panel model or clustered standard errors at the team level",
    "Replicate in the Eredivisie, the Championship, or the Scottish Premiership",
    "Extend to further capped or uncapped leagues (the NFL, MLB, or EuroLeague basketball) for a larger cross-sport sample",
    "Report predictions above Financial Rank 15 with explicit uncertainty caveats, or pool additional seasons to narrow the band"
  )
) |>
  kbl(longtable = TRUE,
    col.names = c("Limitation", "Issue", "Potential Fix")
  ) |>
  kable_styling(
    latex_options     = c("striped", "repeat_header"),
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
    full_width        = FALSE
  ) |>
  row_spec(0, bold = TRUE, background = "#1A2A4A", color = "white") |>
  row_spec(7, background = "#FEF9E7") |>
  column_spec(1, bold = TRUE, width = if (knitr::is_latex_output()) "2.6cm" else "20%") |>
  column_spec(2,              width = if (knitr::is_latex_output()) "6cm"   else "40%") |>
  column_spec(3,              width = if (knitr::is_latex_output()) "6cm"   else "40%")
tbl_cap("Study limitations and suggested future extensions.")
```


# Appendix

## Full Coefficient Table (Models A and H)

```{r full-coef-table, results='asis'}
coef_all |>
  filter(grepl("^[AH]", model_label)) |>
  select(model_label, term, estimate, std.error, statistic, p.value) |>
  mutate(
    across(c(estimate, std.error, statistic), ~ round(., 4)),
    p_display = case_when(
      p.value < 0.001 ~ "< 0.001 ***",
      p.value < 0.01  ~ sprintf("%.4f **", p.value),
      p.value < 0.05  ~ sprintf("%.4f *",  p.value),
      TRUE            ~ sprintf("%.4f",    p.value)
    ),
    model_label = sub(":.*", "", model_label)
  ) |>
  select(-p.value) |>
  kbl(longtable = TRUE,
    col.names = c("Model", "Term", "Estimate", "Std. Error", "t / z", "p-value")
  ) |>
  kable_styling(latex_options = c("repeat_header", "striped"), full_width = FALSE) |>
  row_spec(0, bold = TRUE, background = "#1A2A4A", color = "white") |>
  collapse_rows(columns = 1, valign = "top")
tbl_cap("Full regression coefficient table, Models A (linear) and H (quadratic).")
```

## Model H Quadratic Coefficients, Position Outcome (Detailed)

```{r appendix-modelh, results='asis'}
coef_H_raw |>
  mutate(
    term  = recode(term,
      "(Intercept)"         = "Intercept",
      "Financial_Rank"      = "Financial Rank (b1)",
      "I(Financial_Rank^2)" = "Financial Rank squared (b2)"
    ),
    across(c(estimate, std.error, statistic, conf.low, conf.high), ~ round(., 5)),
    p.value = formatC(p.value, format = "e", digits = 3)
  ) |>
  select(term, estimate, std.error, statistic, p.value, conf.low, conf.high) |>
  kbl(longtable = FALSE,
    col.names = c("Term", "Estimate", "SE", "t-value", "p-value", "CI lower", "CI upper")
  ) |>
  kbs() |>
  row_spec(0, bold = TRUE, background = "#1A2A4A", color = "white")
tbl_cap("Model H (Position explained by Financial Rank plus Financial Rank squared) full coefficient table with 95 percent confidence intervals.")
```

## Position Model Diagnostics: Residual Plot and Heteroskedasticity Tests

Because final league position is still reported alongside points per game in
this report (as corroborating context, not as the primary outcome), the
diagnostic checks recommended for any continued use of an ordinal outcome
are reproduced here in full, rather than only referenced.

```{r appendix-modelh-resid-fig, out.width="90%", fig.height=4.2, fig.pos="H", fig.cap="Residuals vs fitted values, Model H (League Position explained by Financial Rank plus Financial Rank squared). The shaded band marks ordinary variation; labelled points lie beyond 1.5 residual standard deviations."}
knitr::include_graphics("outputs/28_residual_plot_modelH.png")
```

The residual plot shows no obvious funnel shape (residual spread does not
visibly widen or narrow as fitted position increases), which is the
qualitative check a residual plot is meant to provide. Formal tests confirm
what the plot suggests only informally: both heteroskedasticity (unequal
error spread) and autocorrelation (correlated errors across observations,
expected here since the same clubs recur across multiple seasons) are
statistically detectable.

```{r appendix-hsk-table, results='asis'}
hsk_tests |>
  mutate(
    Statistic = round(Statistic, 4),
    p_value   = formatC(p_value, format = "e", digits = 3)
  ) |>
  kbl(longtable = FALSE,
    col.names = c("Test", "Statistic", "Parameter", "p-value")
  ) |>
  kbs() |>
  row_spec(0, bold = TRUE, background = "#1A2A4A", color = "white")
tbl_cap("Breusch-Pagan test for heteroskedasticity and Durbin-Watson test for autocorrelation, Model D (League Position explained by log squad value, league, and season index).")
```

Both tests reject their null hypothesis at p < 0.001: the constant-variance
and independent-errors assumptions behind an ordinary least squares model do
not strictly hold for a position-based specification. This is exactly the
kind of limited interpretability the recommendation to critically analyze
position-based results anticipates, and it is a second, independent reason
(beyond position's ordinal, non-continuous scale) that this report treats
points per game and the generalized additive model as primary, using
position only as corroborating evidence. As a check on how much this
distorts inference, Model D was refit with heteroskedasticity-robust (HC1)
standard errors:

```{r appendix-robust-se-table, results='asis'}
robust_se |>
  mutate(across(c(estimate, std.error, statistic, std.error_OLS), ~ round(., 4)),
         p.value = formatC(p.value, format = "e", digits = 3)) |>
  select(term, estimate, std.error_OLS, std.error, statistic, p.value) |>
  kbl(longtable = TRUE,
    col.names = c("Term", "Estimate", "SE (OLS)", "SE (HC1 robust)", "t-value", "p-value")
  ) |>
  kbs() |>
  row_spec(0, bold = TRUE, background = "#1A2A4A", color = "white")
tbl_cap("Model D coefficients with ordinary vs. heteroskedasticity-robust (HC1) standard errors. Conclusions about which terms are statistically significant are unchanged.")
```

The robust standard errors are close to the ordinary ones for every term,
and no term crosses the conventional 0.05 significance threshold in either
direction as a result of the correction. The substantive conclusions drawn
from the position-based specification are therefore not an artefact of the
detected heteroskedasticity, even though, as a matter of statistical
principle, points per game remains the more appropriate outcome variable
for the reasons given in the Methodology section.

## Points-Per-Game Model Comparison Table (Full Precision)

```{r appendix-gam-table, results='asis'}
gam_table |>
  kbl(longtable = FALSE,
    col.names = c("Model", "Outcome", "N", "R squared / deviance explained", "AIC", "Effective degrees of freedom")
  ) |>
  kbs() |>
  row_spec(0, bold = TRUE, background = "#1A2A4A", color = "white")
tbl_cap("Linear, quadratic, and generalized additive model comparison for points per game, at full precision, from this report's underlying GAM revision output.")
```

## Whole-Project Model Evolution Table

```{r appendix-e1-table, results='asis'}
e1_table |>
  select(Model, Outcome, N, R_squared, AIC) |>
  kbl(longtable = FALSE,
    col.names = c("Model", "Outcome", "N", "R squared", "AIC")
  ) |>
  kbs() |>
  row_spec(0, bold = TRUE, background = "#1A2A4A", color = "white")
tbl_cap("Every position, points, and points-per-game specification fitted across the whole project, from this report's whole-project model evolution output.")
```

## Analysis Script Inventory

The complete, runnable source code for this project is available at
**https://github.com/ShawnMuktadir/Money-and-Success-in-Football**. Readers
who want to reproduce or extend this analysis should start there. The
table below lists every analysis script (scripts 01-12 are in `RScripts/`,
scripts 13-16 are in `extensions/scripts/`), in run order, and what each
one produces.

```{r script-inventory, results='asis'}
tibble(
  Script = c(
    "01_scrape_ESPN_standings.R", "02_scrape_transfermarkt_market_values.R",
    "03_compute_financial_rank.R", "04_merge_standings_and_market_values.R",
    "05_analysis_and_regression.R", "05b_extended_regression.R",
    "05c_model_h4_extended.R", "06_residuals_and_outliers.R",
    "07_beautiful_charts.R", "08_time_split_model.R", "09_points_model.R",
    "10_scrape_nba_data.R", "11_nba_vs_football_comparison.R",
    "12_ppg_inflation_extended.R", "13_model_evolution_comparison.R",
    "14_additional_visuals.R", "15_spal_correction.R",
    "16_linear_gam_revision.R"
  ),
  Produces = c(
    "League standings and points, all five leagues, 2015-16 to 2024-25",
    "Squad market values from Transfermarkt for the same leagues and seasons",
    "Financial Rank computed within each league-season, with proper tie handling",
    "The merged master dataset combining standings and market values",
    "Descriptive statistics and the baseline regression models",
    "Robustness diagnostics, interaction and quadratic model extensions",
    "Model H extended with league fixed effects and a season index",
    "Residual and outlier analysis for Model H",
    "Presentation-ready versions of the residual and outlier charts",
    "Early-decade vs late-decade stability test",
    "Model H re-fit with total points instead of league position",
    "NBA payroll and standings data for the cross-sport comparison",
    "Football vs. NBA salary-cap robustness comparison",
    "Points-per-game outcome and the transfer-market inflation adjustment",
    "Whole-project model evolution comparison table",
    "Follow-up visuals for model evolution and inflation checks",
    "SPAL 2017-18 missing market value correction (Section 3.1)",
    "Linear and generalized additive model specifications for points per game, the primary models used in this report"
  )
) |>
  mutate(Script = if (knitr::is_latex_output()) {
    gsub("_", "\\\\_\\\\allowbreak{}", Script)
  } else {
    Script
  }) |>
  kbl(longtable = FALSE, escape = !knitr::is_latex_output()) |>
  kable_styling(
    latex_options = c("striped", "scale_down", "hold_position"),
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
    full_width = FALSE,
    font_size  = if (knitr::is_latex_output()) 8 else NULL
  ) |>
  row_spec(0, bold = TRUE, background = "#1A2A4A", color = "white") |>
  column_spec(1, width = if (knitr::is_latex_output()) "3.6cm" else "25%") |>
  column_spec(2, width = if (knitr::is_latex_output()) "7.5cm" else "55%")
tbl_cap("Analysis scripts and their outputs, in run order.")
```

## Session Information

```{r session-info}
si <- sessionInfo()
tibble(
  Item  = c("R version", "Platform", "Date knitted", "Key packages"),
  Value = c(
    paste(si$R.version$major, si$R.version$minor, sep = "."),
    si$running,
    format(Sys.Date(), "%Y-%m-%d"),
    paste(c("dplyr", "ggplot2", "broom", "kableExtra", "ggrepel", "mgcv"), collapse = ", ")
  )
) |>
  kbl(longtable = FALSE, col.names = c("", "")) |>
  kbs(bo = c("striped","condensed"), pos = "left") |>
  column_spec(1, bold = TRUE, width = if (knitr::is_latex_output()) "4cm" else "25%")
```

**Package citations.** Wood, S.N. (2017). *Generalized Additive Models*
(2nd ed.), Chapman and Hall/CRC (mgcv). Wickham, H. (2016). *ggplot2:
Elegant Graphics for Data Analysis*, Springer. Wickham et al., *dplyr*
and *tidyr*, https://tidyverse.org. Robinson et al., *broom*,
https://broom.tidymodels.org. Zhu, H., *kableExtra*,
https://haozhu233.github.io/kableExtra/.


*Report generated with R Markdown. Data sources: ESPN FC (league standings),
Transfermarkt (squad market values). All analysis scripts are available in
the project repository. For questions, contact: mdmuktadir0611@gmail.com*
